CURRENT AFFAIRS.

«“We, the undersigned, inhabitants of Newtown-
sandes police district,”” have presented an address to a
‘retired ‘‘ minion’’ of the Britiskk law in Ireland, one
ex-Sergeant Carrick. ‘‘ We, the undersigned,”’ are:—
M. J. Nolan, J.P., M.C.C.; Myles Kearney, Michael
D. Enrigkt, Jeremiah Brosnan, James Walsh, Edmond
Stack, Thomas McElligott. We understand that out of
these seven ‘‘ we, the undersigned,’’ tl{ere are no less
than four Bungs! Yet we see no mention of any hos-
pitality being uncorked in connection with the address;
but then it would seem that bhereiwas no purse_of
sovereigns given away on the occasion. Mr. Carrick
resigned the ¢ Force”” on a pension, and, neye;dless to
say, that according to “ we, the undersigned,” mostly
_composed of Bungs, the sergeant, “although disckarging
your duty with efficiency and zeal, you never on any

_ occasion made your action obnoxious to the people, and

ou always managed to live in harmony with ‘them,
w‘;jl;ch weyl:elieve should be the first care of a guardian
f the peace, and your courtesy in your position enabled

you to obtain information where another would fail to
get it.”” ‘“ We, the undersigned,’’ also ‘ trust in your
retirement into private life you will secure a position
more in keeping with your years and business training,
and that you will live long to enjoy in your
declining years the peace and calm which, on almost all
occasions, were denied you by the onerous duties you
had to discharge whilst in the Police Force.”

After the storm, ‘‘peace and calm!” That is nof
bad surely. Picture the strenuous life of the Kerry
bobby, or the Cork bobby—it will be remembered that
we saw one of them strenuously paddling in a rivulet
some few weeks back. After the hard life of an Irish
country police officer there should come a time of
“peace and calm!”  Might we suggest that after
thirty years or so of the peace and calm and. idleness of
a bobby, it were about time that he shook himself up,
and looked alive. This busy, world-tossed police officer
who tramped the roads of Newtownsandes, in Kerry,
graciously replied to ‘‘ we, the undersigned,” including
the four Bungs. Said he:—‘‘ During tke years which
I had the pleasure of spending in your midst I had on
many occasions experienced your unvarying courtesy,
genial kindness and hospitality.”” Ha! hospitality! And
““ we, the undersigned,”’ contain four Bungs! There is
quite a regal ring about the last paragraph:—“I wisk
you all farewell. My earnest desire shall always be to
hear of the happiness and prosperity of the warm-
hearted people of Newtownsandes.—P. D. Carrick.”

At a meeting of tke Birr Coipoe Ceanncaip, held
tc consider the new rule of the ‘‘ National >’ Board re
Trish as an extra subject in primary schools, the follow-
ing resolution was unanimously passed :—‘‘ That, hav-
ing regard to the vast importance both from a naticual
and educational point of view of the teaching of Irish
n our primary schools, we consider it most unfair and
unwise to relegate it to the inferior position cf an extra
subject of instruction, and contrary to tke practice which
recently obtained, to allow no fees for the teaching of
Irish as an ordinary subject in non-bi-lingual schools.
That should this regulation be adhered to, it must
seriously impede the work of our extern teachers (whose
services are so necessary for the efficient teaching of the
language in the great majority of schools, and so deserv-
ing of remuneration by our educational authorities) by
excluding them from the schools except at-unsuitable
Lours. And that for these reasons we call upon the
Commissioners of National Education to withdraw this
regulation, and allow remuneration for the teaching of
Irish as an ordinary subject in non-bi-lingual as well as
in bi-lingual schools.’”

In the course of the letter accompanying a copy of
{his resolution the Hon. Secretary writes to us:—‘ The
Coipoe consider the withdrawal of this rule of the
greatest national importance. There are ten rural
schools in Birr Coipoe Ceanncaip emong wkich about
600 children are learning the language which is taught
by the Irish teacher employed by the = Coiroe. If
this new rule of the National Board be adhered to, it
will not be possible for any one of these schools to
qualify for fees, as it would not be possible for the
teacher to visit these schools outside tke ordinary school
hours, which are between 10.20 a.m. and 3.30 p.m. -
We surely expected ‘when the' fees were restored our
own language would have its right place in the schools,
but in Ireland we must always be protesting The
Cotpoe will be very grateful if you will call attention -
to this matter in the LEApER, for we know if the
LEADER is on our side our cause is practically won.”

The custom of drinking at threshings was discussed
ab a recent meeting of the Oulart Labour League witk:
& result which we hope will be largely copied. The
following resolution referring to this undesirable prac-
tice was unanimously agreed to:—‘ That we, the mem-
bers of the Oulart Labour League, make known that
we will refuse. to accept drink at threshings or on such
occasions, but instead we hope the persons whom we
are working for will see their way to give as a substi-
tute one shilling for the day.”’

One evening last week we had a visit from our dis-
tinguished contributor, Monsignor O’Riordan, Rector
of the Irish College at Rome. Our readers will be glad
to learn that “M. O’R.” looks in splendid health,
notwithstanding the very serious illness which he
recently put over him in Rome. ‘“M. O’R.” is arrang-
ing for yet another edition—a cheap edition—of his
great book, ‘‘Catholicity and Progress.”

We are glad to read in our contemporary, tke ‘ West-
meath Independent,’” that ‘ the few who are trying to
keep the light of Irish-Ireland burning in Tuilamore

* are determined on making a great effort during the com-
ing winter to make their town as Irish in spirit as it
should be.”” As a step in this direction an Aeridheacht,
under the auspices of the local branch of the Gaelic
League, was held in Tullamore on last Sunday week.
For some years past tLe branch have been accustomed
to hold Aeridheachta in the neighbouring districts,
which, we understand, were uniformly successful and
well attended, in every case the entertainment provided
being value for the money and more.  Apparently,
however, Sunday’s effort in Tullamore was not enthu-
siastically patronised, and our contemporary asserts—
““ For the simple reason that Tullamore is behind the
times of Irish Ireland.””  Our contemporary adds:—
‘“We fear that the greater portion of the population
does not take tlat interest in matters Irish which they
should.  They did not turn up on Sunday in the
satisfactory numbers expected. ~ The loss was theirs,
for they were offered a pleasant means of spending a
couple of the ‘dull hours of a Tullamore Sunday.” At
this rate there is evidently plenty up-hill work before
local Trish TIrelanders. We fear that Tullamore has
plenty company ¢‘ behind the times of Irish-Ireland.’”’
Indeed, so far as Aeridheachta are concerned, we doubt
if it is very muck' behind Dublin.

Tke “Freeman’s Journal’” reported the Munster
Feis every day until Friday last. For on Thursday the
foul and nefarious editor of this journal read a paper on
¢ The Gaelic League in the English-speaking Districts,”’
and so on Friday the poor ‘‘Simply Dwindler”’ said
nothing about the Feis! William O’Brien got excited
over his suppressed speeches, but we are not constituted
that way; we enjoy that sort of advertisement. We
have often noted, as in fairness we were free to note,
the boycott of ourselves by the ‘ truly national >’ Press;
as a matter of fact we took the matter easy and in the
best of humour, for this boycott was one of the most
elcquent tributes ever paid us. It was a great adver-
tisement that a paper that was making the country
jump every week was hoycotted by the * Simply
Dwindler >’ and its contemporaries. At the moment we
don’t know what the profits and the price of the shares
of tke ““ Simply Dwindler’” have dwindled to: even the
mest expert financier must be put out by tke profit and
loss account of the poor ‘ Freeman,” and sure the
shareholders must be very unhappy, too. What a piti-
ful picture!—no wonder the Parliamentary Party, and

The great London Aonach will open on tlys day,
Thursday, in the Royal Horticultural Society’s Hall,
Vincent Square, Westminster, and will continue f,h_e
two following days. In addition to the splendid exhibi-
{ion of Trish industries, there will be many other attrac-
tions, including concerts, plays, dances, ete. Our
readers will notice by an announcement on another
page Messrs. Corrigan and Frencp, 'the_ we]l-kno_wn Te- i

A correspondent wrote :—‘ I enclose you two cuttings
fiom one of our local evening papers, ‘ The Bradford
Daily Argus’ of 19th inst., in which appeared the
reports of police court proceedings here from which
your readers will see that the English correspondents
who recently travelled all the way to Athenry to obtain
sensational ‘copy’ for their ‘great’ English daily
newspapers might not have gone quite so far. There is
abundant copy to be had in some of our English towns.
Please draw the attention of your readers to some of
the ‘virtues’ of the enlightened English, as shown in
reports in question. One can imagine the cry that
would go forth had the occurrences in question taken
place among the ‘ wild Irish.” Tkanking you in antici-
pation, and wishing your paper all success.’’

ST B, T o L SR

One of the reports which our correspondent encloses
concerns the prosecution of a Bradford woman, a mill
hand, who was prosecuted for neglecting her three chil-
dren. A warrant had been issued for the arrest of her
husband, but he had fled. e was a labourer who
could earn 24s. a week, “ but he preferred to work a
little and drink a good deal. In consequence, the
children had for a considerable time past been left to the
mercy of neighbours. The children were verminous;
their clothing was merely an apology, and their boots
were not worth the name. The oldest child had had
clothing given her by the Cinderella Club, but it had
found its way to the pawnshop and the proceeds
had been spent in drink.” Perhaps the ¢ Irish
Times ” would send over a special representative to look
into the state of Bradford! The other cutting dealt
with ‘a miner who was -charged with outraging a girl
child of 3} years of age. The prisoner was committed
for trial to the next Leeds Assizes. Perkaps the ¢ Cork
Constitution * would send over a special representative
also to inquire into the state of Bradford in England!

comes out first amongst the girls’ schools of every deno-
mination in Treland with a total of 39 distinctions,
being 11 more than the next highest. These pupils
i secured cent. per cent. of passes in Irish, and 55 per
cent. of hox_xours in it. St. Louis Convent gained 5

as th main subject, and medals for Domestic Economy,

Commenting on the results of the Intermediate
Examinations, a distinguished educationalist writes to

us as follows:— “ The Results all round this year are

28| most surprising. I have been informed on most excel-
lent authority that the examining in French this year

was, to use a mild word, absurd. No answer was

allowed any mark except practically correct in every

detail.  This rule was most exactingly enforced in

marking the Iionours paper, with the result that

students who took the Honours papers, having read

the Honour Course for their better education—though

French was not a main subject in their course—failed

altogether in tkis subject. - In many cases their failure

f deprived them of a general pass, and in not a few of

A | Exhibitions or prizes. In many of the University
E higher exams., as I'm sure you are aware, especially in

f mathematics, 30 per cent. or 35 per cent. on any result

‘; is considered excellent. The present French examiner,

i however, has different views it seems, and nothing short

f of 90 per cent. with him gets any credit.  This, of

awarded for Irish.
i course, is my own view, and may be exaggerated.”’

We gather that the marking in otker subjects as well
as French is responsible for much dissatisfaction. And
the parsimony of the authorities in cutting down the
number of exhibitions would seem to have introduced
an element of luck into some of the awards that would
be more in keeping with a ‘‘ Limerick > competition
than a great educational test.

‘We have heard of one instance of a boy scoring only
twenty marks or thereabouts below a £40 exhibitioner
being awarded a £3 prize, and of another sixth in Ire-
Jand in his group getting only a book prize.  There
have been probably many other more or less similar
cases of Lard lines. Of course, there must be some
limit on first-class distinctions, but there seems an
abnormally large element of luck about a system that
creates such a gulf between candidates of comparatively
equal merit.

Refezving to the position of Irish in the examinations

a Ligh cducational authority has written us as under:—
“Lhere is the general complaint about the reduction in
the number of exhibitions this year as compared with
last year, but in ‘mo group is, this more keenly felt tlan

* in the one in which Irish is taken as the main subject.
Evidently many of those who take this subject (probably

" Catholics) as a main subject in the lower grades, drop
off altogether in the higher grades. Consequently there
are fewer in this division, and therefore fewer
‘exhibitions given; for the Board =arrange that

Loreto Convent, Wexford, has obtained the ha.ndsome
tetal of seven exhibitions, being the largest ol'Jt:_Lme(_i by
any in the girls’ section, and fifteen other dJ§txnctlons,
including second place in Junior Grade in Irish.  St.
Louis  Convent, ' Carrickmacross, is high up on the list
with seventeen distinctions. There is a coincidence in
the fact that four Loreto Convents—at Letter}(enny,
Rathmines, Kiltimagh and Killarney, respectively—
obtained six distinctions each. Distinctions were also
obtained by, amongst otker convents, Dominican Con-
vent, Blackrock; Sacred Heart Convent, Waterford;
Loreto Abbey, Gorey; St. Mary’s University College,
Donnybrook.

The Ursuline Convent, Waterford; Brigidine Con-
vent, Tullow, Co. Carlow, passed many pupils with
honours in the various grades, and several passes were
also obtained by the Brigidine Convent, Goresbridge.
Several prizes were obtained by the pupils of the Loreto
Convent, Navan, whose successes appear in detail in our
advertising columns. The practical subject of domestic
economy is, we are glad to note, evidently well taught
in this convent. The summary of Inspector’s report,
which is appended to the list referred to, speaks in a
significant manner to this effect.

St. Colman’s College, Fermoy, has, as usual, a big list
of successes, taking first plaec in Ireland in Junior Grade
Mathematical Course along with several other notable
distinctions. The Irish results of this College are con-
sistent with its splendid record. Seventy-eigkt pupils
passed in Irish in the various grades, 38 obtaining
honours in it. Special prizes for Irish composition were
obtaiuned in the Senior and Middle Grades. The per-
centage of passes obtained by St. Colman’s pupils in the
various grades when compared with the general percen-
tage of passes in Ireland is a tribute to the value of an
Irish-Treland education.

St. Eunan’s College;, Letterkenny, scored many dis-
tinctions ; twenty-five of its students passed in Irish, the
great majority of them securing honours marks.  St.
McCartan’s Seminary, Monaghan, where all the pupils
are studying Irish, has five exhibitions and several
prizes to its credit. All the students took Irish, and 81
per cent. passed. Carlow Lay College took three first
places in different subjects, and fifth place in Ireland in
Middle Grade Irish. The Presentation Brothers’ Col-
lege, Mardyke, Cork, has seven medals to its credit for
first places in Treland, including one for Irish.  The
college has a fine record of twenty-one first-class distinc-
tions, and the exhibition, medal, etc., prize list of the
Board. Three exhibitions and several prizes fell to the

lot of pupils of St. Jarlath’s College, Tuam, and it is

appropriate that a college in the Co. Galway should
specially distinguish itself in Trish. One first-class ex-
hibition and two first-class prizes went to Mount St.
Joseph Abbey, Roscrea.

The distinctions of St. Mary’s College, Rathmines,
include two exhibitions and various prizes. Blackrock
College beats Clongowes Wood in the number of exhibi-
tions, but the latter is ahead in the general distinction ‘
results. We are glad to note that pupils in both these
‘colleges won prizes in Irish. .

During the course of Dr. Annie Patterson’s concert
lecture at the Munster Feis, a gentleman sang an Ttalian
song; we understand he was asked to do so by way of
bringing out the contrast between it and an Irish song.
One of the audience thought it was Irish, and remarked :
““There now! There’s Sean O Cuiv and his new
spelling for you!”’

This year’s Munster Feis was a bold venture, and
as one expects of a bold venture when it is boldly
executed it was a striking success. All connected with
it may congratulate themselves. Father Fitzgerald, of
the Cathedral, Cork, and Father O’Brien, of Douglas,
put their backs into tke work, and €amon O ‘Donnacada

kindly presiding at an item in the many-sided function,

himself heartily into the work. The High Sheriff, Mr.
James Dwyer, presided at the paper on the Industrial - = 5

by the Editor of this journal, a journal which has given

tain the idea. We would only ask six mont] A.gsg

The sun was close down on the west now, its red rays
striking through tke willow-shaded foot-track up the
canal. It was the hour of low-spoken words, slow, pen-
sive steps, with perhaps ike glad cry of children at play
breaking in upon the calm. I strolled up towards
Harold’s Cross, past the row of sally trees swaying
gently in the breeze, past little nooks welcome to even-
ing lovers, past those suburban family parties whose
regular evening walk is up this shadowed path, past a
group of boys who were training a 'ittle spaniel to swim
after a stick to a chorus of clear child-laughter, past
two wondering little girls who were absorbed in admira-
tion for an overgrown stickle-back, finally under the
bridge at Harold’s Cross. A step further and I came
across two cheery ragamuffins, bare-legged, unkempt,
but smiling, who wanted to know the time. T told them
¢ ten minutes to nine.”” ‘“ Hlurrah,” cried the bigger
boy, ““only ten minutes more!” “‘Ten minutes more
for what?” T asked. ‘Till we can dip in the canal,”
answered the laddie, looking brightly up at me from
under a tangle of dark curls. Then, with the exube-
rant. confidence of childhood he explained to me that
the “ polis’’ wouldn’t let them bathe till after nine

_o’clock, and that it was middlin’ chilly then, even in
the summer time, but he didn’t mind that. THe was
hardy, so he was. Besides it was only near the bank,
at the first plunge, it felt cold. Once you got into the

. middle it was all right. I looked from my informant to
his companion, a tiny chap in a well-worn sailor suit,
his big grey eyes shining solemnly out on me from the
gathering dusk about his face. ~ ““ Isn’t it dangerous,
bathing in the canal?’ I asked. ‘‘Not if you can
swim,’” said the dark-haired one, adding proudly—‘T
can swim fine ’—and, witl: an air of dignified responsi-
bility—‘“ so I look after this little fellow.”” The little
fellow looked up now, and queried in a babyish treble,
as yet unspoiled by any city twang—‘ Why, mister, do
you want to dip?’ The big boy immediately offered to
show me a good place, wishing, I suppose, to extend
his paternal care to me also, 'and take me under his
wing. But I declined, as graciously as I could. It was
near their long-expected hour now, so they made off to
the place by themselves. I watched the two little
pairs of bare'legs twinkling away into the dusk, and
then turned back to Harold’s Cross. It was not with-
out a pang of regret that I changed the atmosphere of
untamed freshness which seemed to emanate from those
sedgy canal banks for the comparative sophistication of
a Iarold’s Cross-Pillar tram.

The Franciscan Brothers pitched their tents in Mount

pened, was also visiting Glasnevin on that day.

During thirty years there has been a boarding school

ingly low-" musicians" coming ·along who played with

KILKEE.

T stands on a plain which runs out to the broad
I ocean. On either side—north or south—a shelter-
ing hill rises gradually. These two hills' are like
immense outstretched wings that protect, in a measure,
the little valley below from tke winds that in winter
so often disport themselves with no little ferocity
around the coast. At the head of a small semi-circular
bay the cluster of quaint-looking houses range them-
selves together crescent-shaped. Behind a big expanse
of lowland stretches back well beyond the Shannon;
ard, in front, the Atlantic waves roll right up to the
threskold of the town. Thus, sequestered away on the
| western sea-board of Connoae an Cldiy, washed by
the waters of a great ocean stands Kilkee—the prettiest
:and most delightful of sea-side places.

Kilkee is ‘‘ far from the madding crowd.” It is far
from the din and clamour of our advanced, but some-
what nerve-shattering civilisation. To-day, as Zolus
sleeps, and an unclouded sun is prodigal in its genero-
sity, Kilkee nestles down snugly in its own undisturbed
calm, ignoring with a scornful contempt the noise, the
bustling, the impetuousness, the fever of this most
feverish twentieth century.

Peaceful it is, indeed, and uncorrupted. The untamed,
undisciplined crowd of an - English factory city does
not (Durveacar le ’Oia) swarm in to convert by its
very presence a great beauty into a great ugliness, and
-transform by its wild ebullitions a blessed tranquillity
“into a hideous turmoil. Nor is this retreat from the
world’s tumult desecrated by a visitation of that
diabolical product of human society which we term the
““bounder.” In social affairs, artificiality is the essence
- of snobbery ; and since in a Kilkee community nothing
is artificial, you must search hard for the infantile con-
tertions and apish manners of our rather infantile and
apisk friend, the ‘‘ bounder.” Kilkee again is saved
from another abomination (so common amongst English
watering places), for its atmosphere is not yet polluted
“by the inane drivel, the repulsive vulgarity, the lament-
able degeneracy of that institution known as the music
hall. Anglicisation has, of course, penetrated to our
uttermost west, but fortunately it has not brought there
the additional blighting influence of the music hall exhi-
‘bition. No, down here tkey try to get on, and succeed
.admirably in their try, without the assistance of a
~ Tivoli or an Empire. . %
~ Kilkee is, I believe, one of the black spots on the
map of Irish Ireland. To some extent it deserves this
‘characterisation. It is not, however, I am happy to
y, as black as people appear to . think.

cluded Kilkee amongst the seven towns in Munster in

tics in country Ireland at present. You can 
.spend whole months there, in districts, too, that furnish 
their weekly quota of reports of the meetings of this 
political body and that to the local press, and hear 
never a word of politics talked amongst the people. 
The things the people do talk about are principally 
three-the weather, the crops, and, last but not least, 
the state of the labour market. All ·three, be it noted, 
-of interest to them industrially. And as such just the 
things you would imagine the newspapers ought to take' 
.a constant interest in and write about.

wouldn'·t be newspapers if they did.

partly because the people are n9t politicians more than

So much by the way; I intended to write something 
about some of those matters I have spoken of as of 
most present interest to the country people. And first 
about the labour question. 
. 
Like the problem of the £5 worth of books some time 
ago, the labour ·problem in Ireland isn't the same in 
every district of the country. In some parts the farms 
are so small that the farmer doesn't want any labour 
outside that of his qwn family. 
In others, grazing 
ranches instead of farms are the rule, and no labourers 
at all are required. 
I don't happen to have by me the 
figures as to what fraction of the arable land of the 
country is taken up by these two' classes, but there is 
left enough land divided into fair-sized, labourer-
worked holdings, to render the problem of retaining in 
the country sufficient labour to work these holdings 
,economically, one of very great importance. 
Anyone, 
of course, who has read at all regularly the evidence 
given at the various Commissions we have had examin-
ing us this past year or more, is aware of any number 
of plans by which that problem could be soon settled. 
As, however, none of these plans will of a sudden be 
adopted, it may be no harm to speculate, not on what 
would happen if any or all of them were adopted, but 
on what very likely actually will happen in any case. 
Hoping for any good, or, indeed, for anything, to come 
of Royal Commissions is worse than hoping for Home 
Rule every next year. 
The fev,:er "no-f~r-distant-
dates " we keep looking forward to the fewer to-days 
we will permit to pass over our heads unnoticed or 
unprofited by.

And, of course, under

' 
A pleasant outlook this, it will be said, for the Irish 
, farmer! 
Well, the farmers should be fairly well aware 
by this time that they have something of the sort to 
expect. The American fever is their "Black Death," 
and they can't very well at this time of day demand a 
Statute of Labourers tliat would bend the servant to 
the ma~ter's terms under penalty of being branded with 
a hot 1ron. 
There are, however, one or two little 
matters of detail in their treatment of their servants 
that the farmers might with advantage look to, matters 
the mending of which, while it would not settle the 
rural labour question, would be a move towards making 
for them, the farmers, at least the best of a bad story. 
Let me briefly refer to some of these little matters. 
In the first _place, it would- be well if the labourer, 
outside the question of wages altogether-in which 
matter, indeed, he may be depended on to look after 
himself-were treated with the respect due to a respon-
sible human being who is not charged with any crime 
against society in general or the farmer in particular. 
I have suggested that the Irish labourer ought now be 
about the happiest of bis class in the world, but of 
course I only mean. that, considering what his lot until 
quite recently was, be ought to be. 
Speaking abso-
lutely and not merely comparatively, his treatment still 
is such that it is no wonder he is tired of it. In the 
matter of house accommodation alone, the necessity for 
the Labourers' Act, as late as the other day, shows how 
far behind, what any decent state of civilisation would 
presuppose, Ireland is in her treatment of her working 
classes. 
The housing problem is now being attended to, 
but habits are not as easily built as houses, and the 
society that has been accustomed to the old mud cabins 
will, it is to be feared, continue for long years yet· to 
treat the labourers just as if they still lived in the mud 
cabins. 
And this is precisely the sort of thing the 
modern labourer, though possibly he doesn't know it 
himself, is beginning to kick against. 
Ee doesn't 
object to eat his meals off a bare table, nor to sleep, 
when the farmer lodges him, on straw in any sort of an 
outhouse, and he dresses and does all things else accord-
ingly, but when the American letter comes home, and 
he sees his brother washed and clean, in collar and tie, 
"an' phite cuffs down to his knuckles on 'im 
~ ti11c O" in the photo, it doesn't require much know-
ledge of human nature to guess how he feels. 
Self-
respect asserts itself in him, that's what happens. And 
though , when he goes to America: he likely pays dearly 
for his .cuffs and collars and pomted boots-if he has 
them-I wouldn't like to assert that he is sorry for his 
bargain. 
As I say, the old state of society has left in 
us a· certain idea of what was due to the labourer as a 
member of society, and that idea isn't good ~nough for 
the presen~ day. 
But even thoug? it ':"e~e ~ood enough, 
and that we had no.Jabour question, it is m every way 
a thing to be glad of that the labourers_ should be 
developing a spirit of self-respect on the Imes I have 
indicated. 
Indeed, it is the farmer's business, he being 
locally in a position of. certai~ respon~ibility as member 
of his community-besides bemg to his advantage-not 
alone to help the labourer to satisfy his ideas of self-
respect, but to teach him to . develop the self-re~pect 
itself. I needn't go into particulars as to how either 
one or the other could be done; any farmer to whom 
it is any good to be talking, knows how they can be_ 
. done right well himself. 
Another matter is this-perhaps it is the far_mers' 
wives I should ask to note ,it-,-that the present is the 
age of eating whatever you like. 
I kno,! there's no 
use in generalising about the matter--circumstan~es 
alter cases-but where it could be done, I would like 
to try the plan of letting the labourers diet themselves. 
The plan would have two or more advantages over the 
old I believe general system. For one thing, they; 
the' labourers ' would have one matter less to grumble 
at the farmer' about. 
For another thing it would make 
all the world of difference to the farmer's wife and 
daughter fairly free as they now are, since the dairies 
were don~ away with, except for " the m~als." Thirdly, 
it would enable the farmer to pay higher wages m 
money. 
A matter, be it noted, of app~al to the 1:1an 
getting the money; anyone who has noticed how fairly 
easily the smaller landlords can get labour, for no very 
evident reason other than this very one that they don't, 
as a rule, diet their labourers, and so can pay 14s. and 
15s. a week regular wages, may guess that. And we 
all know how big the 18s. a week paid in towns sounds 
to the countryman; he never thinks of th~ gap to be 
eaten out of it. And, as further proof still, see how 
the apparently high wages in America draw our people 
out. Especially as any of the money that is saved and 
sent home counts for twice as much as it would to the 
man that earned it. 
Two pounds a week over there 
with a pound expenses, enables the dutiful son to send 
home a pound. At home on exactly the same terms-
his mother w9uld only get ten shillings. So that like 
the farmer of the old rack-rent days, the labourer of 
to-day is inclined to account nothing to wealth, but 
hard cash. 
Moral : give him nothing but hard cash if 
you wish to most please him. 
A third point is-of course everyone will have thought 
of it-that the farmers should lead the way in support-
ing Irish manufacturers. 
I don't say that they are not 
inclined to do that already-I'm afraid I cau1t say that 
I notice them inclined in any particular way in the 
matter-but I will say that, as far as I know them, 
they don't appear to be very clear as to why they would 
do well to support home manufactures. 
The argument, 
to keep the labour in the country, doesn't appeal to 
them inasmuch as the labour that would be kept 
wouldn't be farm labour. 
Neither do they seem to see 
any advantage in having ·work at home for their own 
redundant sons and daughters, for the simple reason 
that it has not yet entered their economic heads to 
think of putting any of their sons or daughters to such 
things as trades. Neither again do they look forward 
to creating a market in Ireland for Irish farm produce 
-England is Ireland's market they think for all t.ime.. 
And so, considering that these arguments do not, as in 
my experience they don't, appeal to them, I suppose 
it is but little use pointing out that, as America is our 
town, and as that bare fact is the cause, perhaps above 
any other cause, of Irish emigration, it would be a 
good thing for even the Irish farmer to build up some-
thing like up-to-date towns and town-life in Ireland 
itself. 
We can't, of course, hope to rival America, in-
deed we don't particularly wish to, but it is in our 
- power to put some pulsing life in our towns and villages 
and make of them places you could spend a half-hour 
in without falling asleep. 
The farmer's share in the 
doing of that would be to support home manufactures. 
There are many other little points, too many to go 
into now, bearing on this rural labour question. That
